entries.title: South Siders glued to the TV for Jackson's homegoing service: 'Everybody has been watching all day'
entries.summary: <p>Scrambled eggs, grits and sausage patties were Rev. Jesse Jackson’s regular order at Hyde Park’s Valois Restaurant. He’d sit at the fifth table on the right side, without fail. Jackson was a loyal customer, sometimes coming in twice a day, for over 40 years.</p><p>Gianni Colamussi, owner of the popular cafeteria-style restaurant, became friends with the civil rights activist and his family over the years. So streaming Friday’s homegoing services on the television at Valois felt like a good way to honor Jackson’s legacy for Chicagoans who couldn’t make it to the services.</p><p>“He’s been a real big pinnacle of this area and of the restaurant,” he said. “His bodyguard would sit at the table behind him, and if it was just them two, he would eat in peace.”</p><p>The funeral service honoring Jackson began at 11 a.m. at Chicago’s House of Hope, and was attended by thousands. Heavy hitters like former Presidents Barack Obama and Bill Clinton, as well as Rev. Al Sharpton and singer Jennifer Hudson were in attendance.</p><p>But South Side businesses like Valois also streamed the homegoing services for their patrons.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-450000" name="image-450000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7439210/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9224967/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55f9ecb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Rev. Jesse Jackson's favorite table at Valois Restaurant" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55f9ecb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a17cc7b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55f9ecb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff2%2Fe8%2F23a732d0428391914fbc4dd2c63c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson’s favorite table at Valois at 1518 E 53rd St in Hyde Park, Friday, March 6, 2026. Gianni Colamussi, owner of the popular cafeteria-style restaurant, became friends with Jackson and his family over the years. So streaming Friday’s services on the television at Valois felt like a good way to honor Jackson’s legacy.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>During his break, Colamussi, 53, parked himself at the closest table to the TV. The restaurant was crowded with regulars at 11:30 a.m. and most seemed to be going about their business. But Colamussi and a visiting friend, John Pitsadiotis, were fixated on the services.</p><p>Colamussi and his family went to Rainbow PUSH for the two-day lying in state last week. He and his family got “VIP service” that they weren’t expecting.</p><p>“The family had us go straight to the front to pay our respects,” he said. “That was incredible, the respect they showed us was impeccable.”</p><p>Jackson’s brother, Charles Jackson, stopped by Valois a day earlier.</p><p>“We got to talking a little bit, and he said, ‘Man the memories of this place with him, I come here now and it’s eerie,'” Colamussi said.</p><p>Colamussi thinks Jesse Jackson stopped by the restaurant for the last time in the fall.</p><p>“He was slowing down for sure,” he said.</p><div Chandler cuts the hair of customer Arthur Dennis as the shop’s televisions show Rev. Jackson’s celebration of life service.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Over at Hyde Park Hair Salon, longtime barber A.C. Chandler spent the day reminiscing about events with Rainbow PUSH, and about the salon’s time cutting the hair of Jesse Jackson Jr. and Congressman Jonathan Jackson.</p><p>The hair salon, which famously counts former President Barack Obama as a one-time client, streamed the Friday services on both televisions at the salon.</p><p>“Everybody has been watching all day,” Chandler, 46, said.</p><p>Chandler and his client, Arthur Dennis, spent the appointment pointing out people they knew at the funeral.</p><p>“We were just talking about people that we know within our circle that are either active members involved in or part of the Rainbow PUSH coalition,” Chandler said.</p><p>Dennis, a current member of PUSH, said the homegoing celebration was necessary.</p><p>“But also, I think with any celebration of life… It’s about what do you do now? If Jesse Jackson was about solidarity, how are we showing that in our everyday lives,” Dennis, 33, said. “Because it's one thing to show up for a day, but how do you show up for a lifetime?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf30aaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F03%2Fc2fac5c741998f7fa33ad82d4c0c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/79b7e5e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F03%2Fc2fac5c741998f7fa33ad82d4c0c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a38dec4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F03%2Fc2fac5c741998f7fa33ad82d4c0c%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Barber A.C. entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: How Cubs first baseman Michael Busch 'earned' everyday role, at-bats against left-handed pitchers
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. – <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> first baseman Michael Busch’s transition to hitting left-handed pitching will take some level of trial and error.</p><p>Busch, 28, was the left-handed side of a loose platoon for the first two full seasons of his major-league career. He's started consistently against right-handed hitting . But against tough southpaw matchups, the Cubs have always had right-handed hitters – or for parts of 2024, left-handed hitting Cody Bellinger, who has even splits – available to come off the bench or start in his place.</p><p>At the end of last year, the coaching staff discussed getting Busch plenty of reps against left-handed pitching.</p><p>“He's earned it,” hitting coach Dustin Kelly said. “He's had this in his mind for a couple years that he wants to be the everyday first baseman.”</p><p>That was the plan going into spring training, but backup first baseman Tyler Austin was on the roster as a right-handed option off the bench. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/02/25/cubs-tyler-austin-undergoes-knee-surgery-expected-to-be-out-for-months" >With Austin now expected to be out for “months” while recovering from knee surgery</a>, Busch doesn't even have a safety net anymore.</p><p>“Obviously excited about that opportunity,” Busch said recently. “And then I think one thing is just working hard with the hitting coaches, trying to figure out ways mechanically, or even just approach wise [to improve.]”</p><p>Busch only got 95 plate appearances against left-handed pitchers last season. He had a .642 OPS in those matchups, compared to his .910 OPS against right-handers.</p><p>By midseason, however, it became clear that regardless of the opposing pitcher, Busch was often the stronger all-around option over veteran right-handed hitter Justin Turner.</p><p>Busch<b> </b>was in the starting lineup against a left-handed opposing pitcher 10 times last season, eight of which fell in a two-month span from early June to early August.</p><p>At the start of September, the Cubs <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2025/09/01/carlos-santana-relives-memories-of-2016-world-series-as-he-returns-to-wrigley-field-as-a-new-cub" >signed veteran switch-hitting first baseman Carlso Santana</a>, who briefly served as the other side of the first base platoon. But he didn’t make the playoff roster.</p><p>Instead, Busch started all but one postseason game at first base. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2025/10/06/godfather-of-playoff-experience-justin-turner-to-lead-off-for-cubs-in-game-2-of-the-nl" >The only exception </a>was Game 2 of the National League Division Series, when the Brewers chose left-hander Aaron Ashby as the opener for a bullpen day. </p><p>Having Ashby pitch in back-to-back games seemed to be Brewers manager Pat Murphy's antidote for Busch, who led off Game 1 against Milwaukee ace Freddy Peralta with a home run.</p><p>“By the end of the year, it was very interesting how you could feel this flip in how the other team viewed him,” Cubs manager Craig Counsell said this spring. “… The other team was telling you everything you need to know: he’s become a dangerous, dangerous hitter.”</p><p>Busch was the Cubs’ best hitter in the playoffs. He recorded four home runs and a .296 batting average, melding frequent contact and power.</p><p>“In the postseason, there's so many emotions, and so much looking forward to things, or your at-bats,” Busch said. “But trusting your routine was probably the biggest takeaway for me, in that it really does help. It helps you focus when your mind doesn't want you to focus. And so I thought that was pretty cool.”</p><p>Now Busch is in the late stages of a common progression for talented left-handed hitters. Many have to prove themselves in the big-leagues before getting the chance to face lefty pitching.</p><p>Same-sided matchups generally play in the pitcher's favor. But because there are fewer left-handed pitchers, lefty hitters simply get fewer chances to experience those matchups in their baseball lives.</p><p>Teams also have left-handed specialists who have built careers around facing left-handed pockets of the lineup. </p><p>“These past two years, he's murdered righties,” Kelly said of Busch. “So he is the left-handed batter in our lineup that every pitching staff has somebody to go and attack.”</p><p>Regardless of production, Busch’ strike zone awareness has held steady no matter the handedness of the pitcher.</p><p>“So, I didn't really want to toggle with too much,” Busch said. “But trying to find a little something to maybe just help me perform a little better against lefties.”</p><p>Busch has talked through different options with the coaching staff. Then he uses the left-handed pitching machine and the Trajekt machine, which mimics specific pitchers’ arsenals, to experiment.</p><p>“Do we open up a little bit more?” Kelly said. “Do we close off a little bit more? Are we going to try and pick up a release point that's a little bit further on the first-base side so you have to orient your body?”</p><p>So far in Cactus League play, Busch has had six at-bats against left-handed pitchers and recorded two doubles, according to Statcast.</p><p>“There's a time for everything,” Busch said. “There's a time for mechanics, there's a time for approach. There's a time for confidence, there's a time for humility. And then there's a time for testing that stuff.”<br></p>
entries.author: Maddie Lee

entries.title: Top-ranked Benet takes Bolingbrook's best shot and survives
entries.summary: <p>Everything was going against <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/super-25-basketball-rankings/2026/03/01/high-school-basketball-rankings-chicago-illinois-sun-times-michael-obrien" >top-ranked Benet</a> on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/06/ihsa-state-basketball-tournament-scores-illinois" >Friday</a>.</p><p>Bolingbrook’s sold-out gym provided an intense atmosphere. Raiders guard Brady Pettigrew showed the crowd why he’s one of the country’s best sophomores. Senior TJ Williams, a long and athletic 6-3 guard, defended Benet star Jayden Wright better than any player in the past two seasons. And the Raiders were even outrebounding the Redwings, something that hasn’t happened all season long.</p><p>It was the kind of game that very good teams lose on the road in the playoffs. But the defending state champions may be a great team. And they found a way to win.</p><p>Wright battled through the defense, 7-1 Colin Stack made a key pair of three-pointers, and 6-8 junior Ed Stasys came alive in the second half as Benet knocked off No. 8 Bolingbrook 51-44 in a Class 4A sectional final.</p><p>“The first half I wasn’t playing like myself,” Stasys said. “My teammates picked me up and supported me. I had to do what I had to do to win. I had to start pushing it.”</p><p>Stack led the Redwings (34-1) with 14 points, and Stasys added 13 points and seven rebounds.</p><p>“I was proud of our guys just gutting through it,” Benet coach Gene Heidkamp said. “Bolingbrook is a heck of a team. The game was never comfortable, and we were lucky to pull it out in the last minute and a half.”</p><p>Bolingbrook (25-8) led 25-24 at halftime. Stasys had no points and one rebound at the break.</p><p>“Ed did a heck of a job rebounding [in the second half],” Stack said. “He was all over the glass and made sure whoever he was guarding was stopped.”</p><p>A three pointer from Stack at the third quarter buzzer put the Redwings ahead 38-33 and that lead increased to 44-35 on a Stack dunk with 4:59 left.</p><div class="Enhancement" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c2b701/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4591x2577+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2Fbf%2Ffa0e803847a1a955051ec8a201ac%2Fob-cst-030726-007.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Benet’s Jayden Wright (3) controls the ball against Bolingbrook’s TJ Williams (3) during the 4A sectional final game.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kirsten Stickney/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pettigrew asserted himself at that point, scoring or assisting on the Raiders’ next nine points. Bolingbrook cut the lead to two points with 2:11 left but that was as close as it would get.</p><p>Perry Tchiegne, a 6-5 junior, sealed the win with a steal and dunk with 12 seconds left.</p><p>“We have a bunch of great players,” Stasys said. “Perry had a heck of a game. If someone is being taken away, we have other guys that can score.”</p><p>Tchiegne had 13 points and seven rebounds. His offense helped fill the void created by Williams’ impressive defense on Wright, who finished with nine points.</p><p>“[Williams] is an unbelievable defensive player,” Heidkamp. “His length and athleticism really make Jayden work for everything. Nothing was easy.”</p><p>Pettigrew finished with 14 points and 6-7 senior Elijah Anderson added 13 points and six rebounds for the Raiders.</p><p>“They are the No. 1 team for a reason, and they made some plays down the stretch,” Bolingbrook coach Rob Brost said. “We had a couple of opportunities that we missed. But give [Benet] credit. They are good at what they do.”</p><p>Benet will face Rockford Auburn in the NIU Supersectional on Monday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Benet beats Bolingbrook 51-44. Too-ranked Redwings will face the Guilford vs. Auburn winner in the NIU supersectional. <br><br>Stack had 14 and Stasys 13. <a href="https://t.co/iTE262xDiJ">pic.twitter.com/iTE262xDiJ</a></p>— Michael O'Brien (@michaelsobrien) <a href="https://twitter.com/michaelsobrien/status/2030106331571425602?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: Raiders star Maxx Crosby headed to Ravens — not Bears
entries.summary: <p>Maxx Crosby won’t be a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bear</a>. Or a Lion. Or a Cowboy.</p><p>Amid speculation in recent days about the Raiders star edge rusher’s next home, the team on Friday agreed to trade him to the Ravens. The Ravens will send the Raiders first-round picks this year and next, including the No. 14 overall choice this year, per reports. The trade can't be made official until next week.</p><p>Crosby and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/06/bears-trade-garrett-bradbury-patriots-center-2027-fifth-round-pick-nfl" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams shared their mutual admiration in a podcast interview last month. Speaking on “The Rush,” host Crosby told Williams the two were wired the same.</p><p>“What’s gotten you to this point is being Superman, and what’s gotten me to my level of wrecking games is I feel like I’m Superman,” Crosby told Williams.</p><p>That, along with the Bears cutting $44 million in cap space this week because of the retirement of center Drew Dalman, the trade of receiver DJ Moore and the cutting of linebacker Tremaine Edmunds, left Crosby a target for the Bears this offseason. They were even the Las Vegas favorite to do so.</p><p>General manager Ryan Poles warned last week the dangers of going all-in, though, referencing former GM Ryan Pace’s trade for the Raiders’ Khalil Mack and how quickly the Bears’ roster shriveled in the wake of it.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Chicago comes out in force to honor Rev. Jesse Jackson
entries.summary: <p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson was the family member who always showed up — and on Friday, Chicago and much of the country showed up for him.</p><p>Thousands came to the South Side's House of Hope to say goodbye to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" target="_blank" >civil rights leader and two-time presidential candidate</a> who led the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.’s open housing campaign, started the Rainbow PUSH Coalition and traveled the world to free hostages, advise leaders and fight injustice.</p><p>From three former presidents to faith leaders, dignitaries and his family, Jackson was celebrated as a man who instilled change, inspired generations — and was there for those in need. </p><p>Attendees included people like James Hickman, who said Jackson let him sell posters at Rainbow PUSH Operation’s building. Hickman, who lives in Atlanta, said he'd driven about 34 hours to and from South Carolina for Jackson’s funeral services. Friday, he stood outside of House of Hope on the Far South Side selling calendars and a $10 commemorative poster he'd designed himself that outlined the history of Jackson’s life.</p><p>The history each poster tells is so important that he’s willing to give them away if someone can’t pay, he said.</p><p>“I couldn’t go to my local stores and big chains and all that and get our type of information,” Hickman said. “So it’s our job to tell our own stories.”</p><p>Jackson's son, former U.S. Rep. Jesse Jackson Jr. said, "Every single person in here has a Jesse Jackson story. The time he shook your hand. The time he prayed for you. The time he held you up. The time he prayed at the funeral for somebody that you know. The time he was at the hospital for you.”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><h3></h3><p></p><p></p><p>Yusef Jackson said his father spent his last months urging him to mobilize churches to feed those who temporarily lost food assistance during a historic government shutdown last year that froze benefits for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program.</p><p>“I intend to die with my shoes on,” he recalled his father saying.</p><p>At Friday's star-studded and lengthy service, former President Barack Obama, too, recalled visiting with Jackson at a Hyde Park hotel as his health declined, and instead, finding him with a stacked to-do list that never wavered in his final days. Obama and Jackson had a complex relationship, which began with distance on Obama's part as he began his political ascent, but ended in deep admiration. On Friday, he credited Jackson's first presidential campaign in 1984, as well as Mayor Harold Washington's 1983 mayoral run, for "drawing" him to Chicago. </p><div class="Enhancement" class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" >Rev. Jesse Jackson, civil rights leader and Chicago icon, dead at 84</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“I embraced him and figured we’d just have a low-key visit. Maybe he needs some rest,” Obama said of his visit. “And he starts coming up with this project and this initiative and issues I needed to look into. And here’s some commentary that he was suggesting, some phrasing he thought might work, and maybe we might co-write an article, and listening to him, I smiled. Because it took me back," Obama said. "And I started reminiscing about those Saturday mornings at PUSH and the breakfast he’d host away from [the] full meeting."</p><p>Former President Bill Clinton shared a rare, personal story about Jackson calling the White House as Congress worked to impeach him in 1998. Despite an initially rocky relationship, Jackson became an adviser and friend to Clinton, counseling him during the hearings that led to his impeachment by the House and acquittal by the Senate.</p><p>“He said, ‘I don’t want to talk to you. I want you to go get Chelsea [Clinton],” he said. “Keep in mind. He’s got all this other stuff going on. He called me to talk to my daughter, to make sure she had her head in the game. And he prayed with her on the phone. And you know, a lot of people, it would never even have occurred to them to do that.”</p><p></p><p>Clinton, who said he came to the homegoing service more as a “friend” than a former president, said, “those are the things you remember.”</p><p></p><p>“He was my friend when I needed him,” Clinton said.</p><p></p><h3>‘He was our neighbor’</h3><p></p><p>Jackson advised presidents, met with kings and queens “and dictators and clergy of all the great religions,” Gov. JB Pritzker told the crowd. Shortly before the Democratic National Convention in July 1984, Jackson embarked on a tour of Central America. In Nicaragua, he condemned the financing of anti-Sandinista insurgents by Ronald Reagan’s administration. In Cuba, he met with Fidel Castro, negotiated the release of 22 American prisoners and brought them back to the United States.</p><p></p><p>“But here in Chicago, he was our neighbor. He was our friend,” Pritzker said. “We were so proud. We are so proud. ... While we shared him with the world, Rev. Jackson belonged to Chicago, and Chicago belonged to him.”</p><p></p><h3></h3><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><h3></h3><p></p><p></p><p></p><h3>‘How do you show up for a lifetime?’</h3><p>Scrambled eggs, grits and sausage patties were Jackson’s regular order at Hyde Park’s Valois Restaurant. He’d sit at the fifth table on the right, without fail. Jackson was a loyal customer, sometimes coming in twice a day, for over 40 years.</p><p>Gianni Colamussi, owner of the popular cafeteria-style restaurant, became friends with Jackson and his family over the years — and streaming Friday’s services on the television at Valois felt like a good way to honor Jackson’s legacy.</p><p>“He’s been a real big pinnacle of this area, and of the restaurant,” he said. “His bodyguard would sit at the table behind him, and if it was just them two, he would eat in peace."</p><p>During his break, Colamussi parked himself at the closest table to the TV. The restaurant was crowded with regulars at 11:30 a.m., but he and a visiting friend, John Pitsadiotis, were fixated on the services.</p><p>Over at Hyde Park Hair Salon, longtime barber A.C. Chandler spent the day reminiscing about events with Rainbow PUSH and about the salon’s time cutting the hair of Jackson Jr. and U.S. Rep. Jonathan Jackson.</p><p>The hair salon, which famously counts Obama as a one-time client, streamed the Friday services on both televisions at the salon.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-720000" name="image-720000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71da92e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cee35f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88d6e1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Barber A.C. Chandler cuts Arthur Dennis hair at Hyde Park Hair Salon as the newscast of Jesse Jackson Sr’s funeral is played on tv in Hyde Park, Friday, March 6, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88d6e1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/caf03b7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88d6e1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F27%2Fce%2Faec9daf147b5899bba181fd1f5f9%2Fjjhomegoingremote-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Barber A.C. Chandler cuts Arthur Dennis’ hair at Hyde Park Hair Salon as the newscast of Jesse Jackson funeral is played on TV in Hyde Park Friday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Everybody has been watching all day,” Chandler, 46, said.</p><p>He and his client, Arthur Dennis, spent the appointment pointing out people they knew at the funeral.</p><p>“We were just talking about people that we know within our circle that’s either active members involved in or part of the Rainbow PUSH Coalition,” Chandler said.</p><p>Dennis, a current member of PUSH, said the homegoing celebration was essential to mourn someone so important to the city.</p><p>“But also, I think with any celebration of life. ... It’s about what do you do now? If Jesse Jackson was about solidarity, how are we showing that in our everyday lives?” Dennis, 33, asked. “Because it’s one thing to show up for a day, but how do you show up for a lifetime?”</p><h3>Honoring what Jackson stood for</h3><p>Alecia Washington lives and serves on the city council for Stonecrest, Georgia, but grew up in Chicago, surrounded by Jackson’s programs like Operation Bread Basket. She traveled back for the services Friday. “I definitely had to be here to make sure that I gave my final respects to Rev. Jesse Jackson,” she said.</p><p>She will most remember Jackson for the work he did to shape the Black identity after the Jim Crow era.</p><p>“My parents struggled, they came from the South,” Washington said. “They came up here and tried to make a living so we all flocked to that movement.”</p><h3></h3><p>For Benita White Arnold, Friday’s celebration of Jackson’s life marked a moment in history. That’s why she made sure her 15-year-old grandson joined her. She joined 25 other members of East Chicago, Indiana’s NAACP 3046-B branch in attending the service at House of Hope. She saw a parallel between the civil rights movement Jackson helped lead — and today’s activism.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b20000" name="image-b20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/21c7f43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44ae681/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a0da50/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Members of an NAACP branch from East Chicago, Indiana wait to get into the House of Hope on the South Side for Jesse Jackson's service Friday. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a0da50/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b8d4f1d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a0da50/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F60%2F60d8fe4543a688a49062b700f0b5%2Fimg-6514.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Members of an NAACP branch from East Chicago, Indiana wait to get into the House of Hope on the South Side for Jesse Jackson’s service Friday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Elvia Malagon/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We got to remember how we got here and fight like hell to move this needle forward,” she said. “We owe this to him.”</p><p>White Arnold said she will continue registering people to vote and spread awareness. “Most importantly, I’m saying something,” she said.</p><h3></h3><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><h3>Jackson, ‘a very special fallen brother’</h3><p>Before the service began, Thomas Finch stood outside House of Hope proudly wearing his Omega Psi Phi jacket and displaying his large, billboard-sized video screen that would later broadcast the event for those still waiting to enter. Finch said more than 300 of his fraternity brothers attended a special memorial service the fraternity held Thursday night for Jackson, a member of Omega since 1960. "It's a significant ritual that we have for fallen brothers, and Rev. Jackson was a very special fallen brother. It was very moving. It was really a celebration. We love him and his legacy will live on."</p><p>Finch, who owns Finch Video Services, has been in business for 30 years. But he said it was hard to get his company off the ground. He said Jackson pressured big organizations to allow room for small business owners get a foot in the door.</p><p>"I couldn't get into hotels like the Hilton, the Hyatt, McCormick Place," Finch said. "Jesse Jackson knocked down some doors. I credit him with my business going up over 30 years. I'm now doing NASCAR. I've done presidential celebrations for Obama. I've done two Democratic conventions. I've done the dedication for Martin Luther King's memorial out in Washington, D.C. And one of the big events I did was the Million Man March and Bro. Jesse Jackson was with us at that. Without my big screens, it's a million men fighting because you couldn't see anything."</p><p><i>Contributing: Neil Steinberg, Lynn Sweet</i><br></p>
entries.author: Alden Loury | WBEZ

entries.title: 3 presidents, and thousands of everyday people, celebrate the life and legacy of Jesse Jackson
entries.summary: <p>In life, the Rev. Jesse Jackson sought out the powerful with tireless intensity.</p><p>In death, the powerful sought him out, one last time, as three former U.S. presidents and a galaxy of luminaries paid tribute to the civil rights leader Friday on the South Side. </p><p>Former Presidents Barack Obama, Bill Clinton and Joe Biden, former Vice President Kamala Harris and former first ladies Hillary Clinton and Jill Biden were joined by thousands of celebrants in a five-hour public homegoing service at the House of Hope, 752 E. 114th, a 10,000-seat facility in the Pullman neighborhood.</p><p>Obama brought up the country's divisive climate under the Trump administration and praised Jackson's voice of inspiration, calling on Americans to become "heralds of change." Many speakers spoke to this being a moment for the country not to despair, but to have faith and take action, as Jackson would have done. </p><p>Each day, Obama said, brings “some new assault on our democratic institutions, another setback to the idea of the rule of law, an offense to common needs.</p><p>"Each day we’re told … to fear each other, to turn on each other and that some Americans count more than others, and that some don’t even count at all. ... Everywhere, we see greed and bigotry being celebrated, and bullying and mockery masquerading as strength," he said.</p><p>Obama said it’s “tempting for some to compromise with power” or for Americans to simply put their heads down.</p><p>“But this man,” Obama said, pointing to Jackson’s casket, “Rev. Jesse Louis Jackson, inspires us to take a harder path. His voice called on each of us to be heralds of change, to be messengers of hope, to step forward and say, 'Send me wherever we have a chance to make an impact, whether it’s in our schools, our workplaces, our neighborhoods, our cities, not for faith, not for glory or because success is guaranteed, but because it gives our life purpose.'"</p><p>For Obama, that inspiration came from Jackson's 1984 presidential run. He described being a college student and sitting in a "janky apartment" in New York as he watched Jackson debate his opponents on TV. </p><p>"Jesse hadn’t just held his own — he had owned that stage," Obama said. "... And the message he sent to a 22-year-old child of a single mother, with a funny name — an outsider — was that maybe there wasn’t any place, any room, where we didn’t belong.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/09076b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/135a551/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4950dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Former President Barack Obama speaks during the funeral service honoring Rev. Jesse Jackson at House of Hope on the Far South Side, Friday, March 6, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4950dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c9d1dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4950dd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5801x3256+0+307/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Ffd%2Fbbde778d41d88c29e507bc06c9d7%2Fjackson-030726-45.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former President Barack Obama speaks Friday during the funeral service honoring the Rev. Jesse Jackson at House of Hope on the Far South Side.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Jackson’s ‘courage of his convictions’</h3><p>The celebration resounded with gospel music, and Jennifer Hudson belting out "A Change Gonna Come." Biden told the gathering that Jackson was "underrated, undeterred and unafraid."</p><p>"We had very different backgrounds, and in some cases different views, but never on race," Biden said. "Sometimes we went toe-to-toe and disagreed about issues. But that's what I admired most about Jesse: his passion, the courage of his convictions."</p><p></p><p>Clinton said he appeared at the House of Hope "more as a friend than a former president," adding that he had met Jackson at an anniversary celebrating the integration of Central High School in Little Rock, Arkansas.</p><p>"I came to truly love Jesse Jackson," Clinton said. "We both had more in common than we thought. We were two guys born to single mothers, living in the South.</p><p>"We did not always agree, but he made me a better president. He was always pushing on things."</p><p></p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson, as well as former Mayors Lori Lightfoot and Richard M. Daley, attended, along with his brothers Bill Daley, a former Obama chief of staff, and Cook County Commissioner John Daley.</p><p>“People ask me what Jesse Jackson meant,” said Lightfoot. “Just look at this. ... Everyday Chicagoans felt he was their advocate.”</p><p>The Chicago service was the second-to-the-last stop in a funeral odyssey that echoed Jackson's life in constant motion — and included a stop in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/02/the-rev-jesse-jackson-greenville-south-carolina-colombia-greenville8-lying-in-state-chicago" >South Carolina,</a> where he was born. U.S. House Speaker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/02/20/house-speaker-mike-johnson-rev-jesse-jackson-family-request-lie-in-honor-us-capitol" >Mike Johnson refused </a>the Jackson family's request that Jackson lie in honor in the Capitol rotunda in Washington, a stark reminder that the nation's commemoration of a civil rights icon comes at at a time when the Trump administration has been actively suppressing Black history.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a28a221/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3779x3779+944+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe1%2Fec%2Fbc18e977482487da60c17695cee1%2Fjackson-030726-73.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘You are somebody’</h3><p>Detroit Pistons Hall of Famer Isiah Thomas recalled growing up in the 1960s looking up to Jesse Jackson as a civil rights hero. </p><p>"We had just come out of Jim Crow," Thomas said. "When society was telling me I was a nobody, when society was telling me we don't even want to go to school with you, this man walked up to me and my mom ... and then he did the unthinkable. Mama Jackson, your husband kneeled down, and he looked me in the eyes, and that man said, 'You are somebody.'"</p><p>Thomas cried as he recalled how Jackson comforted his family when his mother died. "He sent my momma off, beautiful words. When we were at our lowest, he lifted us up on those broad shoulders."</p><p> The celebration of Jackson's 84-year life was strong on style, with many attendees decked out in their Sunday best or funeral black. But there were a few baseball caps, and Mr. T., the 1980s television star, was there in in American flag coveralls. </p><p></p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker also spoke, beginning his remarks by quoting the Bible: "Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like a mighty stream. For Rev. Jesse Jackson each day was a new opportunity to bring justice in a too-often unjust world. His ambition was to shape a world where justice was not an anomaly, but a constant."</p><p>"While we shared him with the world, Rev. Jackson belonged to Chicago, and Chicago belonged to him," the governor said.</p><p>Biden also turned to Scripture, saying, "Jesse believed the Bible tells us that they that wait upon the Lord, shall renew their strength," Biden said. "They shall mount up with wings of eagles. They shall run and not be weary."</p><p>Johnson called Jackson " a brilliant strategist, an organizing savant.</p><p>“It was his instincts that understood that labor and faith were one and the same as Dr. King prophesied. It was his instincts to stand up against school closures and the shuttering of mental health clinics. It was his instincts that led protests down Michigan Avenue when a boy’s life was taken by a police officer and the government tried to cover it up. ... It was his instincts to run in ‘84 and ‘88, but not in ‘92, to make way for somebody in the Deep South and a brother on the South Side to become president of the United States,” Johnson said, referring to Clinton and Obama. </p><p></p><p>There was humor, too. Rabbi Steven Jacobs joked about Jackson arriving at the Pearly Gates and being met by God, who exclaims, "Oh my God, you're Jesse Jackson! You <i>are</i> somebody!"</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f80000" name="image-f80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a19d612/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6993ce2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1935ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jennifer Hudson sings during the funeral service Friday honoring the Rev. Jesse Jackson at House of Hope on the Far South Side." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1935ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f8c6239/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1935ff9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4431x2487+0+234/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F28%2F05f639644e9abf3f3eddd9bc2bce%2Fjackson-030726-039.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jennifer Hudson sings during the funeral service Friday honoring the Rev. Jesse Jackson at House of Hope on the Far South Side.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘A beautiful remembrance’</h3><p>President Donald Trump was invited but did not attend, nor did the other living president, George W. Bush.</p><p>Harris, one of the last speakers, described Friday's celebration of Jackson's life and legacy as "a beautiful remembrance of his spirit, his life, his faith." </p><p>The former vice president said the day had renewed "her faith in what is possible"</p><p>"As a child, I was raised, when you see a closed door you should knock and wait to be invited in. What life has taught me is if that invitation is not offered, and that door remains shut after repeated attempts to knock on said door, sometimes you have no choice but to kick that door open," Harris said. "Rev. Jackson was impatient. He did not waste time waiting, even when the doors in front of him were barred and bolted. ... He always devised a way through."</p><p><i>Contributing: Tina Sfondeles</i></p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Cubs pitcher Ben Brown 'hell-bent' on bouncing back from up-and-down 2025
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> right-hander Ben Brown has sat through long drives to each of his Cactus League appearances this spring.</p><p>On Friday, it was halfway across the valley to Peoria for a night game. Last weekend, it was to Glendale. But his first start of the spring was the farthest commute, more than an hour to Surprise.</p><p>‘‘It’s exciting to start new and to see your name on a lineup card again,’’ Brown said after that start Feb. 23, in which he pitched two scoreless innings against the Royals. ‘‘I feel extremely blessed just to have this opportunity to go out there and pitch, and I’m thankful for it. So there were a lot of emotions driving over here today, just a lot of excitement. I’m grateful for new beginnings, new starts, and I felt awesome.’’</p><p>Brown entered his start Friday with a spotless spring ERA and only three hits allowed in four innings. He struggled, however, against the Padres, allowing three runs and three hits with three strikeouts and three walks in 2 2/3 innings.</p><p>Spring training is just the beginning of a nearly nine-month slog through 162 regular-season games and, ideally, a deep postseason run. For Brown, however, it’s also a check point after a intensive offseason program.</p><p>‘‘I’ll give Ben credit,’’ manager Craig Counsell said. ‘‘The day the [2025] season ended, he had a plan.’’</p><p>The way Brown describes it, it was ‘‘all-encompassing,’’ covering mental, mechanical and physical aspects to go with changes in his arsenal. He spent much of the offseason working with Mets right-hander Clay Holmes, pitching coach Tommy Hottovy said.</p><p>Brown entered spring training more physically imposing. He has been throwing a new sinker in Cactus League outings while continuing to work on his changeup.</p><p>‘‘I was taking all accountability for where things went wrong last year, ultra-accountability,’’ Brown said. ‘‘It wasn’t anyone’s fault but mine how things went. And I wanted to identify all the issues I had. And I wanted to make the best out of them and work on them and learn from them and really have an opportunity to show I can do more, I’m capable of more. And I was hell-bent on doing that.’’</p><p>Hottovy has taken it upon himself to guide Brown back to some of the positives from last season, as well. Along with the lows, Brown had some dominant starts against strong lineups. And though he fell out of the rotation and finished with a 5.92 ERA, he also achieved a major goal of his: staying healthy.</p><p>‘‘And now you use that to springboard this season,’’ Hottovy said.</p><h3>Cubs at Padres</h3><p>Only one of the Padres’ three runs against Cubs starter <b>Ben Brown </b>came on a hit. The first scored on a bases-loaded walk and the third on a wild pitch. They were the first runs Brown had allowed this spring.</p><p>• As World Baseball Classic pool play got underway, several Cubs made notable contributions to their national teams. Right-hander <b>Javier Assad </b>pitched 3 2/3 scoreless innings for Mexico in an 8-2 victory against Great Britain.</p><p>• Outfielder <b>Seiya Suzuki</b> scored both times he reached base to help lift Japan past Chinese Taipei 13-0 in a game cut to seven innings by the mercy rule.</p><p>• Closer <b>Daniel Palencia </b>pitched a hitless ninth inning and struck out two for Venezuela in a 6-2 victory against the Netherlands.</p><p>• <b>On deck: </b>Athletics at Cubs, 2:05 p.m. Saturday, Mesa, 104.3-FM, <b>Mason Barnett </b>vs. <b>Shota Imanaga</b>.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" entries.author: Maddie Lee

entries.title: Arike Ogunbowale arrested after punching man at Miami nightclub, according to police
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — Dallas Wings star Arike Ogunbowale was arrested and charged with misdemeanor battery after police say she punched a man in the face at a Miami nightclub.</p><p>A four-time WNBA All-Star, Ogunbowale was celebrating early Thursday at the club E11EVEN after winning the Unrivaled championship with the Mist that night. According to Miami-Dade County police records, Ogunbowale punched the man in the face, knocking him to the ground, and security cameras captured the act.</p><p>The county corrections department said Ogunbowale has been released from custody.</p><p>"The league is aware of an incident involving Arike Ogunbowale and we are in the process of gathering additional information," Unrivaled spokesperson Tish Carmona said. "We're in contact with Arike and her representatives."</p><p>The Wings said they were aware of her arrest and "are in the process of gathering more information. Further comments will be provided once we have more details."</p><p>The Associated Press left message seeking comment with Excel Management, which represents Ogunbowale.</p><p>Ogunbowale scored 19 points in the title game Wednesday night against the Phantom.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.title: White Sox lefty Anthony Kay recovers from rough start to throw 3 scoreless innings
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. — It was another one of those starts for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> left-hander Anthony Kay. He opened the bottom of the first by loading the bases with Diamondbacks with one out before gutting it out.</p><p>Luken Baker struck out swinging and A.J. Vukovich lined out to left. No runs, no harm, no foul.</p><p>“I just struggled to get in a groove very early on,” he said near the end of Chicago’s 6-0 win. “But thankfully I was able to settle in and not give up any runs. I was able to lock in during the second inning.”</p><p>Final line: Three innings, two hits, no runs, two walks, two whiffs, 51 pitches. A nice spring training outing heading in the right direction.</p><p>“I think I was trying to do a little bit too much,” he added. “I was overthrowing a bit. I talked with [Zach] Bove and Bobby [Hearn] in the dugout, and they just settled me down. Just worry about throwing strikes and not trying to be too nasty. Just let them hit it a little bit.”</p><p>Keep in mind, Kay hasn’t pitched in the major leagues since 2023 with the New York Mets. He’s spent most of the last three seasons in Japan. He’s glad to be back.</p><p>“Yeah, for sure,” he said. “Japan is great, but it’s not the big leagues. I’m really excited to be here,” after signing a two-year free-agent contract with the White Sox guaranteeing him at least $12 million.</p><p>There was a lot of development in Japan, he added.</p><p>“I had to learn to be a starter again. I had to work on my command over there, for sure. I definitely learned a lot about myself and really regained a lot of confidence over there.”</p><h3><b>Contract signings</b></h3><p>The White Sox signed one-year contracts with 31 of their 32 pre-arbitration eligible players on Friday, renewing the contract of infielder Miguel Vargas when he didn’t come to terms. According to a club spokesman, Vargas was renewed at $805,700.</p><p>Among the top paid were pitchers Tyler Gilbert at $900,000, and Shane Smith at $825,900, and infielder Lenyn Sosa at $803,550.</p><p>The amounts paid to the remaining signees were not disclosed. All players on the 40-man roster are now signed for the 2026 season.</p><p>Here is the remainder of the list:</p><p><b>Pitchers:</b> Alexander Alberto, Prelander Berroa, Sean Burke, Ky Bush, Jonathan Cannon, Duncan Davitt, Brandon Eisert, Wikelman González, Jordan Leasure, Davis Martin, Tanner McDougal, Chris Murphy, Jedixson Páez, David Sandlin, Grant Taylor, Drew Thorpe and Mike Vasil.</p><p><b>Catchers:</b> Korey Lee, Edgar Quero and Kyle Teel.</p><p><b>Infielders:</b> Brooks Baldwin, Curtis Mead, Chase Meidroth Colson Montgomery, and Tanner Murray.</p><p><b>Outfielders:</b> Luisangel Acuña, Everson Pereira and Tristan Peters.</p><h3><b>Roster moves</b></h3><p>After Friday’s win over the D-backs, the Sox made 10 roster moves as promised beforehand by manager Will Venable.</p><p><b>Optioned to Class AAA Charlotte </b>: RHP Duncan Davitt.</p><p><b>Reassigned to minor-league camp:</b> RHPs Mason Adams, Adisyn Coffey, Tyler Davis, Zach Franklin and Jairo Iriarte, LHP Shane Murphy, C Michael Turner and INFs Tim Elko and Jacob Gonzalez.</p><p>With the moves, the White Sox have 57 players remaining in camp: 30 pitchers, five catchers, 11 infielders and 11 outfielders.</p><h3><b>Sox 6, Diamondbacks 0</b></h3><p><b>• Sox </b>manager<b> Will Venable </b>said he saw the highlights of Friday’s WBC game from Tokyo where Japan wiped out Chinese Taipei, 13-0, in a match that ended after seven in the Tokyo Dome because of the mercy rule.</p><p> White Sox first baseman Munetaka Murakami drew a walk to open the second inning. Japan loaded the bases for Shohei Ohtani, who thrilled the sellout crowd by hitting a grand slam into the right field bleachers.<b> </b>Japan scored 10 runs in the inning and Murakami scored three runs in the game.</p><p>“He’s been in the middle of the action,” Venable said. “I think those guys playing in that atmosphere, they’re all going to benefit from that.”</p><p><b>• Jared Kelenic</b> hit a three-run, fourth inning homer off D-backs right-hander Joe Ross, his first of the spring.</p><p><b>Three Sox relievers</b> combined to shutout what amounted to an Arizona minor league team for the final six innings, allowing two hits, no walks and striking out five.</p><p><b>• On deck:</b> Sox at Mariners, 2:10 p.m. Saturday, Peoria, CHSN, 1000-AM, <b>Grant Taylor</b> vs. <b>Logan Gilbert</b>.</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: Blackhawks run by kids now after trading entire leadership group: 'It’s their locker room now'
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' old guard is long gone. The Hawks' placeholder veterans of recent years are now mostly gone, too, following the trade deadline.</p><p>The kids run this team now.</p><p>"It’s their locker room now," general manager Kyle Davidson said Friday. "It’s time for them to take over and say what needs to be said, when it needs to happen."</p><p>This week, Davidson did something historically unprecedented by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/04/blackhawks-oilers-trade-jason-dickinson-colton-dach-draft-pick" target="_blank" >trading</a> all three official members of the Hawks' leadership group: Nick Foligno, Connor Murphy and Jason Dickinson.</p><p>And it wasn't even surprising. All three were pending free agents who saw their exits coming well in advance. This was always the plan.</p><p>For the portion of the fan base fed up with the excruciating slowness of this now four-year-long rebuild, this is the moment they ought to start paying attention again.</p><p>The young generation the Hawks have spent all this time gathering — which Davidson believes will carry the franchise back to glory — is now assembling in the NHL. That process was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/09/21/blackhawks-gm-kyle-davidson-fair-judgment-nhl-prospects" target="_blank" >already underway</a>, but it feels like a new chapter officially began this week.</p><p>"I don’t fear for the lack of leadership," Davidson said. "I think it’s there. It’s just going to come in a different form now. I’m excited for a lot of those young guys to find that voice."</p><p>That generation will of course be led by Connor Bedard, who will take over as an alternate captain (along with veteran Tyler Bertuzzi) for the rest of this season and will likely be named permanent captain next season.</p><p>"Coming into this season, we obviously saw [Bedard's] play, but his voice and ability to command that room...really took a step forward," Davidson said. "You feel it when you’re around him. You see it when the group is just hanging out around the rink or on the road. He's a guy who people gravitate toward, and I believe he’ll continue to grow in that respect and find his own leadership style."</p><p>The other bricks are starting to be put in place around Bedard, as well. Frank Nazar, Oliver Moore and Ryan Greene were already here. Anton Frondell will arrive later this month. Roman Kantserov and Nick Lardis should be full-time NHL players next season. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Davidson said the Blackhawks will test out several young players in the 3C role now that Dickinson is gone.<br><br>Moore and Greene obviously will be two of them.</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2030055698441425177?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 6, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Some prospects within the group of Sacha Boisvert, AJ Spellacy, Marek Vanacker, Vaclav Nestrasil, John Mustard, Jack Pridham, Mason West and more should also end up hitting, even though not all will.</p><p>The Hawks do still have some veteran forwards left — Bertuzzi, Ryan Donato, Teuvo Teravainen, Andre Burakovsky and Ilya Mikheyev (the one notable pending UFA <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/06/blackhawks-hold-on-to-ilya-mikheyev-past-trade-deadline" target="_blank" >not dealt Friday</a>) — but their days are all numbered to various degrees.</p><p>Eventually, the Hawks' forward group will resemble their current defensive group, which now features just one player older than 24. </p><p>On the right side, Murphy's departure leaves the trio of Louis Crevier, Artyom Levshunov and Sam Rinzel. On the left, Alex Vlasic and Wyatt Kaiser are poised to become the vocal leaders of the back end.</p><p>"I definitely don't want to come across as somebody who's just stepping up because ... Murphy leaves," Vlasic said this week. "But I'm going to pick my spots and try to figure out how to help lead this team with what I do know."</p><p>Davidson did shift his tone slightly Friday in regards to using his massive stash of picks and prospects to trade for established NHL players. </p><p>He clarified that's not exclusively a "down-the-road" idea but rather something they've already explored and attempted. Blues star Robert Thomas, who didn't end up moving Friday, is one option they objectively should consider, for example.</p><p>"I would hope and expect, in the future, to add and inject more talent," Davidson said. </p><p>"But we can only deal with what we know, and we know all these players coming up. We believe ... wholeheartedly, not just with rose-colored glasses, that they can get us to where we want to go."</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Will Venable taking his sweet time assembling White Sox' pitching staff
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> manager Will Venable knows more than anyone that it’s high time for him and his staff to make some sense out of their pitching staff with less than three weeks to go until Opening Day at Milwaukee on March 26.</p><p>Right now, he has a closer — Seranthony Dominguez — but no Opening Day starter and nothing in between. Shane Smith, Anthony Kay, Sean Newcomb, Sean Burke and Davis Martin have made the most of their recent starts. Four others are being stretched out just in case.</p><p>It’s a process story, and Venable has been asked almost every day where he is in that process.</p><p>“We’re two days closer than the last time you asked,” he said, perhaps a bit sarcastically.</p><p>But come on, there has to be a list, some kind of pecking order in evaluating these pitchers.</p><p>“That is a good point and that part of it is real,” Venable explained. “We have a number of guys who are in competition for a starting rotation job. At some point we need to give them some information so they can adjust their routines and gear toward long relief roles or whatever they might be doing.</p><p>“It’s coming here soon. But there are still tough decisions we have to make.”</p><p>Those decisions couldn’t come too soon. Right now, the potential starters have been rolling with it, but the point of no return is quickly approaching. Plus, there’s nothing to say that changes couldn’t be made in April after the rush toward Opening Day.</p><p>Burke said Thursday night after a hiccup start against the Guardians he feels secure in his bid for a starting rotation berth.</p><p>He allowed four runs on four hits to the first six batters he faced: Angel Martinez, Brayan Rocchio and George Valera opened with a trio of doubles. With two out, CJ Kayfus hit a two-run homer. After the rough start, he resettled and retired seven of the last eight through three innings.</p><p>At this point, it’s not what you really want.</p><p>“I believe I should be in the starting rotation,” Burke said. “I learned a lot last year from the ups and downs I went through. Working with the new coaches and talking through some stuff, I feel super confident going into the year.”</p><p>To be fair, it’s not as if the Sox have a marquee starter. They haven’t had one since Garrett Crochet was traded to Boston after Chicago’s record 121-loss 2024 season. Bud Black, who influenced Venable during their days as manager and player in the Padres organization, said Friday he has one way of building a pitching staff.</p><p>“Get a No. 1 starter — an ace — and a premium closer,” Black, also a fair major-league pitcher and pitching coach in his own right, said in response to a text message. “Then fill in from there.”</p><p>“Yeah, that sounds good to me,” Venable said.</p><p>The Sox aren’t there yet, but Smith could be the ace.</p><p>Smith, Davis and Burke made a combined 76 starts for the White Sox last year. They were 18-29. Kay played in Japan for the past three seasons, going 9-6 with a 1.74 ERA in 24 starts last season for Yokohama.</p><p>He signed a two-year contract with Chicago during the offseason that guarantees him $12 million, is loaded with performance bonuses and has a mutual $10 million option for 2028 with a $2 million buyout.</p><p>Newcomb pitched out of the bullpen last year for the Athletics, is 33 and hasn’t started regularly since his first two seasons with Atlanta.</p><p>It’s a staff without substance and texture.</p><p>“That’s a big thing for us: not to care about what people are thinking outside of this clubhouse,” Martin said. “Face it, our staff is going to look different, than like say, the Dodgers. It’s not going to be as much copy and paste like other organizations because of where we’re at.”</p><p>But it shouldn’t be worse than, say, the Rockies for that matter, either. Watch this space.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, count=9736, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, items=[{type=Image, _styledTemplate=null, media=[{image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/40/ab/005a3fb04986a48daf0331e0aa8b/st26-cws-venable-01-f10.jpg, width=4200, height=2800}, alt=ST26-CWS-venable-01-f10.jpg, narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/40/ab/005a3fb04986a48daf0331e0aa8b/st26-cws-venable-01-f10.jpg, width=4200, height=2800}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs}], category=[{items=[White Sox]}], imageAlignment=null, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, isFeatured=false, title=Will Venable taking his sweet time assembling White Sox' pitching staff, anchorId=promo-fc0000, wordCount=650, target=null, primaryMediaType=null, publishDate=March 06, 2026 06:38 PM, authors=[{authorPageUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/barry-m-bloom, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, jobTitle=[], _template=/author/byline/AuthorByline.hbs, name=[{items=[Barry M.
