entries.title: Horoscope for Saturday, March 07, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Scorpio.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You may be the warrior of the zodiac, but you are also the hero or heroine. No question. Perhaps your heroic presence will contribute to making this day one of the most romantic of the year for you. Don’t hesitate to buy something alluring to wear. (You’re gorgeous.)</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a popular time for you, in part because old friends from the past are back in your world. Today is a very sweet, sentimental and romantic day and for many of you, it's an opportunity to explore a secret flirtation or clandestine love affair. (I'll never tell.)</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You make a wonderful impression on others today, which is fortunate because this is a romantic day and you are making the most of it. In fact, a friend might become a lover. A boss, someone older or an authority figure might also be in the picture.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Someone is attracted to you today, while many of you are dabbling in a flirtation with a boss or someone in a position of authority. This person might be different or from another culture or from far away. The promise of travel looks sweet.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an incredibly romantic day, and you might be thinking of someone far away or someone who is different or from another culture. You might also be involved with the property or wealth of someone else. Some of you will stay at home today.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Romantic relationships today are not only passionate; they are also sweet and affectionate. In fact, all your relationships with partners, spouses and close friends will be bolder and more direct today. You won't hesitate to speak your mind and share your feelings.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a happy day for you because it's easy for you to look upon those who are closest to you with starry eyes and admiration because Venus is conjunct Neptune directly opposite your sign. This might create an illusion — but it will be a happy illusion.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon is in your sign, dancing with Mars, which will energize you and boost your confidence. You might even take over a situation or lead a group of people because you are literally radiating an inner excitement. Meanwhile, a work-related romance might be percolating.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Although there is a lot of energy pulsing at home, especially because relatives might be camping out on your doorstep, the romantic possibilities for you today are quite spectacular. You might develop a crush on someone. New love can blossom.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a busier, fast-paced day, and you feel the need to share your ideas and feelings with someone. Meanwhile, relations with family members are sweet and affectionate. You might also reach out to a friend today or a member of a group, especially if you feel flirtatious.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> People notice you today and see you as competent and strong. What they might not notice is a more sensitive side of you, which appreciates the beauty of your daily surroundings. This "sensitive person" is also more willing to share and divulge their affections for someone. Ah, sweet romance!</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You feel strong and energetic today because different possibilities are available to stimulate your sense of adventure. Because you are a sensitive, romantic sign, you might go overboard buying something luxurious for yourself or someone special. (The price of love?)</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Rachel Weisz (1970) shares your birthday today. You are idealistic, practical, sensitive, empathetic and intellectual. You're also determined to achieve your ambitions. This is a slower-paced year. Take time to rejuvenate your energy. Focus on your business and personal relationships and make sure you're dealing with people who are kind, helpful and have your back.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Blackhawks' defense struggles early in post-deadline loss to Canucks
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> will try out a couple youngsters in the third-line center role vacated by Jason Dickinson during the stretch run, general manager <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/06/blackhawks-trade-deadline-reaction-run-by-kids-kyle-davidson-connor-bedard" target="_blank" >Kyle Davidson</a> said Friday.</p><p>Oliver Moore technically got the first crack at it in a 6-3 loss against the Canucks. Ryan Greene should also get a look at some point.</p><p>"[There are] some exciting opportunities for those players to jump into, grab hold of and show that they can perform in," Davidson said.</p><p>With the Hawks dressing 11 forwards and seven defensemen to lessen the individual burden on each young defenseman, the forward lines ended up being in constant rotation. Still, that's a role Moore believes might suit him well long-term.</p><p>"I don't know if this is what they want me to do, but I love shutting other guys down," Moore said Thursday. "I think I could use my speed in those areas defensively and try to press on teams."</p><p>Moore's performance was a mixed bag. He got confused about his coverage assignment after losing a defensive-zone faceoff in the first period, and the Canucks scored seconds later. Coach Jeff Blashill mentioned that as one of several "ups and downs."</p><p>Three shifts later, however, Moore earned a primary assist on a one-touch pass to set up an pretty Ilya Mikheyev goal. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">GOAL: Moore feeds Mikheyev who deposits for his 12th goal of the season. <a href="https://t.co/E0XIv11HBk">pic.twitter.com/E0XIv11HBk</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2030103505587917047?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Moore generated several more scoring chances in the second and third periods, as did the rest of the Hawks. Perhaps the best came off the stick of Hawks debutant Andrew Mangiapane with five minutes left. They finished with a 30-22 advantage in chances, per Natural Stat Trick.</p><p>But Canucks goalie Nikita Tolopilo made a heroic toe save on Mangiapane's look and held the fort until his teammates buried two late empty-netters, securing the Canucks just their third win in 24 games. </p><p>Allowing that to happen at the United Center isn't a great look for the Hawks.</p><p>"Coming out of the break, we were excellent defensively for four games," Blashill said. "Tonight, we weren’t good enough defensively at all early. It showed. You get down 3-1. After that, we played pretty well; I didn’t think we gave up a whole bunch. But...we can’t expect ourselves to be a team that’s going to win 5-4."</p><p>Frank Nazar scored his first goal since Jan. 30 (and his third since Oct. 28). Backup goalie Arvid Soderblom (16 saves on 20 shots) wasn't great in net after Spencer Knight fell ill during the day.</p><p>As a team, the Hawks won only 18 of 55 faceoffs, which are going to be a weakness sans Dickinson and Nick Foligno with such a young group of centers.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: I stood up to bullies as a teen. Now, I join — and invite — others in standing up to Trump
entries.summary: <p>Dirty snow was piled along the curb. It was a school day in Evergreen Park that late January, already getting dark at 4 p.m. I had just finished delivering newspapers to patients at Little Company of Mary Hospital, subbing for my brother, who was the regular paper boy.</p><p>A 17-year-old never gets enough to eat, and I walked across 95th Street to Sherwood’s Drive-In for a little something to hold me over till dinner. A 20-cent cheeseburger with ketchup and a pickle with a 10-cent cup of coffee with powdered cream would do the trick. I stepped up to the counter to place my order.</p><p>Before anyone came to wait on me, three older looking boys slammed open the storm door and crowded the other register window. The ostensible leader, with brown eyes, dark hair and a black woolen overcoat, placed both palms on the brushed aluminum countertop.</p><p>"You," he said, looking at the lone clerk through the opening in the partition. "Two burgers, mustard, pickle. Make it [expletive] good."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-030000" name="module-030000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>I had seen the boy before at the bowling alley on Rockwell Avenue, a brawler known around the village and to local police.</p><p>As a kid with five brothers who all played hockey and football, I was no shrinking violet. Though I generally avoided trouble, I was the only other customer in Sherwood's and had a feeling of dread. The alarmed expression on the face of the sole employee did not help.</p><p>The brawler, tapping his fingers on the counter, suddenly turned and faced me.</p><p>"I need 50 cents. You got 50 cents?”</p><p>I should have said no, but I could not bring myself to lie. In retrospect, I might have framed my answer differently. </p><p>"Not for you," I said.</p><p>He stared as if noticing me for the first time. He turned back to his order, which had been placed on the counter in front of him. Next, he picked up the white bag and handed it to his partner. Then he lunged.</p><p>It was not as bad as I expected. He didn’t lead with a fist to my eyes or nose but shoulder-tackled me in order to pin me to the ground, where he likely hoped to do more damage. He could not know, however, about me and my brothers, and he felt no stronger than James, the second oldest of us boys.</p><p>We rolled around on the gritty concrete floor, and I had hold of him as much as he had me. Then, his other partner, the one not holding the white bag, squatted and pulled him away.</p><p>"Cops are comin’, Frank."</p><p>We both got to our feet, and he glared at me another second, a flicker of a smile and they bolted. It was then that I noticed the Sherwood’s employee standing in the back by the doorway, holding a black phone receiver.</p><p>Growing up in the '60s, we had to avoid, navigate and occasionally defend ourselves against the dozen or so teenage toughs living in "the Park" who were known for fighting.</p><p>These bullies may have been born with aggressive inclinations. Or it’s possible they had been poisoned as toddlers by gnawing on window sills covered with lead paint, which has been known to cause brain damage and an increase in anti-social behavior in survivors.</p><p>Maybe they were simply good at it, the way one might be talented in a sport like football or boxing.</p><p>Or perhaps they had training similar to Donald Trump’s, who was raised by a taskmaster of a father who taught him that there were only <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/interview/mary-trump/" target="_blank" >two kinds of people</a> in the world, winners, who he called “killers,” and losers.</p><p>Trump would complete his education in bullying at <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/article/trump-the-bully-how-childhood-military-school-shaped-the-future-president/" target="_blank" >military school</a>, where he reportedly pushed weaker boys around and perfected a lifestyle employing force and insult.</p><p>His subsequent development is now documented in American history: mocking enemies with offensive nicknames and threatening all his perceived opponents, including universities, law firms, entertainers, politicians, political parties,and heads of state. He's also since used his the war powers at his disposal to take another nation’s lunch money, i.e., Venezuela’s oil.</p><p>And now we have his lifetime signature act of bullying in what <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbur.org/news/2026/03/02/massachussetts-iran-trump-democrats-republicans-views" target="_blank" >Democrats</a> and even some Republicans consider an unnecessary <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/03/01/chicagoans-iran-middle-east-ties-worry-united-states-israel-continue-bombing" >war with Iran</a>.</p><p>Despite his past anti-war, anti-interventionist rhetoric, Trump has succumbed to his individual lust for power and dominance in violently attacking a weaker nation, at the expense, already, of hundreds of innocent lives, and of the economy and stability of the entire world.</p><p>I learned back in my Evergreen Park days that you have to stand up to a bully, whether alone or, preferably, with the community’s help.</p><p>Likewise, Americans and the world community must stand up to Trump with continued resistance via the courts, Congress, United Nations, free press, protests and ultimately, the polls.</p><p><i>David McGrath is an emeritus English professor at College of DuPage and author of “</i><a class="Link" href="https://www.amazon.com/Far-Enough-Away-David-McGrath-ebook/dp/B0CPNSTWMG#:~:text=Book%20overview,are%20not%20Irish%2C%20after%20all" target="_blank" ><i>Far Enough Away</i></a><i>,” a collection of Chicago area stories.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-380000" name="module-380000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: David McGrath

entries.title: Rev. Jesse Jackson wasn't perfect, but his influence can't be denied
entries.summary: <p>Over the years, there have been several moments when I found myself cringing when others spoke about the Rev. Jesse L. Jackson Sr.</p><p>I’ve often been taken aback by the criticisms.</p><p>Some complained that he was always seeking the cameras, sticking his nose in matters where it didn’t belong. Some railed against the reverend, saying he was always talking about racism or speaking of problems that had been solved. And others criticized Jackson, saying he was soaking up the spotlight and not allowing others to raise their voices.</p><p>Some critics would cite mistakes he’d made in his personal and professional lives — things he shouldn’t have done and words he shouldn’t have said.</p><p>For sure, Jackson wasn’t a perfect man. But I always found it hard to digest that kind of talk.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d70000" name="module-d70000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>In grammar school and high school, I learned of the words and the works of many iconic leaders of the Civil Rights Movement, most notably those of Malcolm X and the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. I often wondered what it must have been like to experience seeing them and hearing them in real time. I imagined an aura around them as they confronted a nation, spoke with passion and energized millions more to join the fight.</p><p>As I learned of those historical Black icons, another one, Jackson, was being shaped and molded right before my eyes in my hometown. Soon he, too, ascended to first-name status, especially in Chicago.</p><p>The generation before me had Malcolm and Martin. I had Jesse. And in time, he developed that same aura that I imagined of other civil rights icons before him.</p><p>Coming of age in Chicago in the 1970s and 1980s, it was impossible to ignore Jesse. Even before he became a household name, his message and his mission captivated me.</p><p>His words — and the ways in which he delivered them — have been inspiring. His message that we’re not defined by our circumstances still resonates with me today. Over the years, I’ve leaned on those words during times of hardship, particularly in my youth, growing up in a single-parent home. And I’ve found his boldness an affirming comfort in pushing back against stereotypical narratives about Black people and speaking out against racial injustice.</p><p>The framing by which I came to understand the struggle for Black liberation and equality in America — and the ways to address it — was shaped, in part, by Jackson’s voice and his actions. I’ve had the honor of speaking with him directly during roundtable discussions about the modern-day struggles facing Black communities — the same struggles they’ve always endured in one form or another.</p><p>Jesse always connected the dots, tying our past to our present by linking the openly racist practices of yesterday to the inequitable policies of today. And he engaged directly with leaders of politics, business and community — alerting them of the consequences of their actions and forging deals and alliances to provide remedies.</p><p>Before Harold, Michael, Oprah and Barack became Chicago institutions, there was Jesse. And while some may not share the same love for Jackson as they do for Washington, Jordan, Winfrey and Obama, the meaningful impact Jackson has had can’t be denied. As has been detailed in numerous stories following his death, the footprint of Jackson’s work and influence was massive. He’s touched the lives of so many people in this city, this country and across the world.</p><p>The lives of both Martin and Malcolm were cut short by assassins. Jesse lived longer than the two of them combined, but his character has been too often assassinated.</p><p>The worst things I’ve heard are the accusations that Jackson was a con man, profiting from a myth that racism is still alive. But those critics are wrong. The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s helped change laws and ushered in a new era of consciousness about our nation’s most challenging subject: race. But the goals of the movement have not yet been fully achieved. The playing field is still not level, and the venom of prejudice is still very much alive.</p><p>The fight for liberation and equality for Black people continues, as it does for other people of color, women, the LGBTQ+ community, Jewish people, Muslims and people living with disabilities. At various points in his career, Jesse fought for all of them.</p><p>In this moment, as Jackson is laid to rest, let’s also lay the criticisms of him to rest. Instead, let’s reflect on his message, let’s honor his mission, and let’s pick up the mantle.</p><p>There’s still so much work to be done.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/alden-loury" target="_blank" ><i>Alden Loury</i></a><i> is data projects editor for WBEZ and writes a column for the Sun-Times.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-770000" name="module-770000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Alden Loury | WBEZ

entries.title: Illinois arts funding shouldn't be treated as an afterthought
entries.summary: <p>In Illinois, lawmakers debate tax freezes for a state-of-the-art stadium. In Indiana, legislation advances to create the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/26/bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-hammong-senate-passes-bill-27-arlington-heights-george-mccaaskey-kevin-warren-nfl" >Northwest Indiana Stadium Authority </a>to lure the Bears across state lines. Public officials in both states are assembling incentive packages and long-term financing strategies, with estimates suggesting up to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/26/chicago-bears-property-tax-incentives-illinois-house-city-opposition-arlington-heights-proposed-stadium" >$855 million</a> in indirect public support.</p><p>When a professional team signals movement, government responds with urgency.</p><p>Across Chicago, a different negotiation is unfolding — quieter but no less important. Small and midsized arts organizations are negotiating leases, absorbing rising costs and operating without permanent infrastructure or predictable public investment.</p><p>For years, the Chicago Latino Theater Alliance has convened and supported Latino theater companies across the city. Many operate itinerantly, rehearsing in borrowed or rented spaces. Sets are built to be struck quickly. Seasons are shaped as much by space availability as by artistic intention.</p><p>These are professional artists producing year-round work without long-term stability. They employ actors, designers and administrators and serve audiences across neighborhoods — yet they do so without consistent growth in public funding.</p><p>The aspiration is not a billion-dollar complex. It is stability: a permanent cultural home where companies can rehearse without watching the clock and productions are not dictated by rental calendars.</p><p>There is no interstate bidding war for that vision.</p><p>Each year, the Chicago Latino Theater Alliance provides grants ranging from $3,500 to $10,000 to small Latino theater companies participating in Destinos: Chicago International Latino Theater Festival. That support helps cover stipends, rehearsal space and production expenses, stabilizing investments that ensure productions move forward.</p><p>But philanthropy cannot carry this alone.</p><p>In Gov. JB Pritzker’s proposed 2026–2027 state budget, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/02/23/pritzker-budget-arts-funding-flat-illinois-2026-2027-grants-trump" >arts funding remains flat</a>. In an inflationary environment, flat funding amounts to a reduction in real support, as rent, labor and production costs rise. At the federal level, National Endowment for the Arts <a class="Link" href="https://glasstire.com/2026/01/21/u-s-congress-passes-bill-fully-funding-nea-and-neh-through-2026/" target="_blank" >funding</a> remains modest and politically uncertain.</p><p>Decades of <a class="Link" href="https://www.brookings.edu/books/sports-jobs-and-taxes/" target="_blank" >economic research</a> suggest publicly financed stadiums rarely deliver the transformative growth often promised. Meanwhile, arts and cultural production contributes <a class="Link" href="https://www.arts.gov/news/press-releases/2025/arts-and-cultural-industries-grew-twice-rate-us-economy-adding-12-trillion" target="_blank" >more than $1 trillion</a> annually to the U.S. economy — over 4% of gross domestic product, according to federal data.</p><p>If public officials can mobilize with such fierce dedication around a stadium, they can do the same for cultural institutions that operate year-round, employ local workers and strengthen neighborhoods.</p><p>Culture, too, is infrastructure.</p><p><i>Jorge Valdivia, executive director, Chicago Latino Theater Alliance</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bf0000" name="module-bf0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Volunteering isn’t a fleeting activity</h3><p>I didn’t like Jane at first. She was blunt, sarcastic, openly irritated by anyone who approached her with practiced concern. The first time I spoke to her, she didn’t look up. She just said, loud enough for others to hear, "Oh great. Another one."</p><p>When I sat beside her, she sighed. "What do you want?" Not, "Can you help me?" Just, "What do you want?"</p><p>I was working in a shelter for the unhoused, still new to outreach and still believing that kindness and effort were enough. I thought people pushed help away because they didn’t really want it.</p><p>Jane used sarcasm as armor. She had learned how to expose fake compassion and deflate people who came believing they were different. So she tested me.</p><p>"Why are you talking to me?" "What makes you think you can help?" "Oh, please — spare me."</p><p>Every sentence dared me to leave. What I didn’t understand then was that Jane wasn’t trying to push me away. She was trying to find out if I would stay.</p><p>At the time, I still believed helping meant fixing. I thought kindness would be enough. I didn’t yet understand what it meant to sit with someone whose life had taught them not to trust anything that came easily.</p><p>One day, after an especially biting comment about "rookies who think they’re saviors," something in me shifted. I didn’t defend myself. I didn’t walk away.</p><p>I stayed. Quietly. Steadily.</p><p>She looked at me — real eye contact, for the first time — and said, "Huh. Interesting."</p><p>From that day on, pieces of Jane surfaced through the cracks: a memory here, a fear she didn’t name, clarity tangled with confusion. She knew when her thoughts weren’t trustworthy. That awareness didn’t empower her — it haunted her.</p><p>One day, she told me, "You’re not listening like the others. You’re actually here. You might be good at this ... if you don’t quit."</p><p>It wasn’t praise. It was a warning. Jane didn’t soften for me. She didn’t change because of me.</p><p>I changed because of her. She taught me that helping isn’t about being liked. It’s about returning the next day after being pushed away the day before. About letting someone challenge your patience without taking it personally. Before you can help, you have to be willing to stay.</p><p><i>Donna Lomelino, Springfield</i></p><h3>A ‘special relationship’</h3><p>The phrase <a class="Link" href="https://www.instituteforgovernment.org.uk/explainer/us-uk-special-relationship" target="_blank" >“special relationship” </a>has often been used to describe the relationship between the U.S. and the United Kingdom.</p><p>Actually, it is a better fit to describe the relationship between the U.S and Israel.</p><p>The U.S. gives <a class="Link" href="https://www.cfr.org/articles/us-aid-israel-four-charts" target="_blank" >more military aid to Israel </a>than any other country. As set by law, annual appropriations amount to $3.8 billion per year. In addition, emergency funds have been allocated far exceeding this amount.</p><p>As the military action against Iran illustrates, intelligence-sharing and military cooperation dramatically reveal how closely aligned each country is to each other.</p><p>Also, while the Trump administration says it opposes Israeli annexation of the occupied West Bank, it <a class="Link" href="https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/24/us-to-provide-consular-services-in-illegal-israeli-settlement" target="_blank" >hasn’t take any steps to curb</a> these incursions that violate international law. </p><p>While Trump has often championed the notion of making <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/trumps-remarks-on-canada-becoming-the-51st-state-raise-a-lot-of-questions" target="_blank" >Canada the 51st state</a>, the “special relationship” between the U.S. and Israel and the fact that the U.S. has been more supportive of Israel than any other country, actually illustrate that Israel is as close to being a state as you can possibly get without a formal process being advocated and implemented.</p><p><i>Larry Vigon, Jefferson Park</i></p><h3>Neighborhoods aren’t ‘saved’ with less housing</h3><p>Chicago’s <a class="Link" href="https://news.wttw.com/2025/11/06/wall-street-ratings-agency-sounds-alarm-about-chicago-s-finances" target="_blank" >dire</a> financial position will keep getting worse without drastic action. Making it easier to build homes and expand the city’s tax base is critical to addressing this budget gap. As housing costs continue <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagobusiness.com/residential-real-estate/housing-affordability-improves-big-cities-not-chicago" target="_blank" >rising</a>, Chicago must make more homes. Failing to build new homes steals opportunities from families and pushes higher costs of living onto current and future Chicagoans.</p><p>The problem is exacerbated by <a class="Link" href="https://chicagoyimby.com/2025/01/a-breakdown-of-aldermanic-prerogative-as-old-town-canvas-is-blocked.html" target="_blank" >aldermanic prerogative</a> and deference to community groups that <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2025/10/29/former-sheffield-neighborhood-group-members-say-its-process-favors-insiders/" target="_blank" >rarely reflect</a> the neighborhood’s actual composition. Frustratingly, even when solutions are approved, another issue exists: neighbors filing lawsuits.</p><p>On the North Side, two different groups are suing to overturn city zoning decisions. An old adage about litigation says that "If you can’t win on merit, then you try to win on procedure." This fits the lawsuits from the 1660 LaSalle Condominium Association in Old Town and the Edgewater Residents for Responsible Development.</p><p>In July, the 1660 LaSalle Condominium Association <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/07/15/old-town-condo-association-lawsuit-developer-fern-hill-36-story-residential-tower" target="_blank" >filed suit</a> against the city and the developer of Old Town Canvas, a 349-home <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.urbanize.city/post/city-council-approves-old-town-canvas-1600-n-lasalle" target="_blank" >development</a> near multiple CTA stops. Despite three years of community engagement, the lawsuit claims that the city "usurped the community engagement process" — although <a class="Link" href="https://chicagoyimby.com/2025/04/old-town-canvas-approved-by-city-council-after-years-of-reviews.html" target="_blank" >nearly a dozen</a> public meetings took place and remain <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/@fernhillcompany/videos" target="_blank" >viewable<u> </u></a>online.</p><p>In October, the City Council <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/news/chicago-city-council-vote-taller-buildings-more-housing-broadway-edgewater-uptown/" target="_blank" >approved</a> the Broadway Land Use Framework, permitting more housing along a busy commercial corridor in Uptown. The <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/sites/broadway-land-use-planning/webinar_presentation_13125.pdf" target="_blank" >plan</a> aims to spur new development nesr four recently <a class="Link" href="https://www.transitchicago.com/rpm/whatsnew/" target="_blank" >modernized</a> CTA stations. During the yearlong community engagement <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/sites/broadway-land-use-planning/home.html" target="_blank" >process</a>, Edgewater Residents for Responsible Development formed as opposition to the land use plan, <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2025/09/18/broadway-rezoning-fight-intensifies-in-edgewater-with-some-neighbors-taking-out-billboards/" target="_blank" >purchased billboards</a> decrying the proposed changes and filed a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/01/13/edgewater-residents-businesses-sue-chicago-broadway-zoning-changes" >lawsuit</a> in January, claiming the city “failed to give proper notice” of the zoning changes.</p><p>In these cases, each group claims to be "saving" their neighborhoods: Both include the word "save" in their website domains. If Chicago’s recent budget <a class="Link" href="https://news.wttw.com/2025/12/20/city-council-votes-30-18-approve-final-part-2026-budget-will-mayor-veto-it" target="_blank" >fiasco</a> were any indication, saving our city means growing our tax base.</p><p>The plaintiffs may believe they are "saving" their neighborhoods, but their litigation harms Chicago by denying new housing and raising the cost of living. To truly save our neighborhoods, the first step should be dismissing these lawsuits with prejudice. When community groups litigate to block housing, it is a form of generational theft against current and future residents.</p><p><i>Josh Chodor,</i> <i>Urban Planning and Policy graduate student, University of Illinois Chicago</i></p><h3>Desk decor</h3><p>Harry Truman had a sign on his desk that read <a class="Link" href="https://www.trumanlibrary.gov/education/trivia/buck-stops-here-sign" target="_blank" >“The Buck Stops Here.”</a> Donald Trump needs one that reads "The Bull— Starts Here".</p><p><i>Don Kolodziey, Richton Park</i></p><h3>Trump’s logic to start war</h3><p>Wow, my approval rating is way down. What to do? Americans support a wartime president. Who can I invade? Iran looks good. Preapproval by Congress ? Hah! That's a laugh. My people will follow me lock-step. What comes after? I don't care. I can't worry about worldwide chaos. I think I could use a Big Mac.</p><p><i>Philip S. Witt, Northbrook</i></p><h3>Tired of Trump</h3><p>I am tired of waking up every morning wondering what new damage and/or embarrassing action and/or words will come from our president. It is exhausting!</p><p><i>Carole Harrington, Bartlett</i></p><h3>Know-nothing Noem </h3><p>Kristi Noem testified that she <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/03/dhs-kristi-noem-senate-judiciary-committee-testimony-marimar-martinez-dick-durbin" >knows nothing</a> about Marimar Martinez, the Chicago woman who was shot five times by gun-happy Border Patrol thugs.</p><p>Does she read the newspapers? Does she watch television news — even Fox "Phony News?" Is she on the internet? Does she listen to her subordinates?</p><p>This is the woman who shot her dog because she was unable to train it. And she was our Homeland Security secretary?!</p><p><i>Dan McGuire, Bensenville</i></p><h3>Tips for Ticketmaster </h3><p>It was 11:30 a.m. on Feb. 20. I was seated at my desk on my computer waiting for <a class="Link" href="https://www.ticketmaster.com/bruce-springsteen-and-the-e-street-tickets/artist/860453?landing=c&awtrc=true&c=SEM_TMMCONCERTS_ggl_23581234228_192116752303_bruce%20springsteen%20ticketmaster&GCID=0&gclsrcshybbb=aw.ds&gad_source=1&gad_campaignid=23581234228&gbraid=0AAAAAD_KsMIloamz2NacAz2l79yiPsSP9&gclid=CjwKCAiAqprNBhB6EiwAMe3yhk2WzM3Y-lAVJXQthXP02Hy-2nFOxZi5fn5rX2eeTWzR-NEv08DYjBoCaXsQAvD_BwE" target="_blank" >Bruce Springsteen</a> tickets at the United Center to go on sale at noon. I was ready. I was quickly notified that I was in a "virtual waiting room." I got excited, hoping to get two, possibly four seats.</p><p>The clock struck noon. I was informed that I was number 27,312 in line. The "bots" have won again. Do Ticketmaster officials care? I think not. They also sell after-market tickets from "scalpers" on the Ticketmaster website — another way to pick your pocket.</p><p>If they were serious about thwarting bots, here's what they could do, although I am not naive, and I know that they will never do this: <br></p><ol class="rte2-style-ol" id="rte-1219bda1-1334-11f1-b6c9-693938b9c72a" start="1"><li>Offer a maximum of two tickets per order.</li><li>Require ticket purchasers to attend the show with a photo ID.</li><li>Require the purchaser to send ID information of the second person attending the performance during the purchase procedure.</li><li>Require that second person to bring the same ID to gain entrance to the concerts.</li></ol><p>These actions would solve many problems. This would frustrate ticket brokers while selling tickets to fans who actually want to go to the concert. </p><p>Shame on the performers for not demanding changes. Sadly, it appears as if they don't care who attends their show either.</p><p><i>Jerry Bernacchi, Rolling Meadows</i> </p><h3>Sun-Times’ Rev. Jesse Jackson coverage informative </h3><p>Thank you for the daily and extensive coverage of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" >Rev. Jesse Jackson’s</a> life and impact. I am a Sun-Times newspaper subscriber, so I saved the special Feb. 18 section and have savored every additional article.</p><p>Although I am of Jackson's generation and have admired him from the beginning, your <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/18/lights-camera-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-pop-culture-presence" >coverage</a> helped me learn so much more. I carry an exuberant memory of his speech at the 1984 Democratic National Convention. Thank you. He was somebody!</p><p><i>Kay Whitlock, Lake View East</i></p><h3>Chicago institution proves pivotal </h3><p>Many years ago, I attended a performance by the late 12-string guitarist and vocalist Glen Campbell at Old Town School of Folk Music. It changed my life. </p><p><i>Scott Schada, Glenview</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Gift shop Transit Tees looks to launch new products, sell more on Amazon
entries.summary: <div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4c0000" name="module-4c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b><i>Editor’s Note: </i></b><i>Last year, a new presidential administration stepped in with plans to disrupt the way companies do business through new policies, an effort to spur growth.</i><br><br><i>To help gauge the administration’s impact, the Chicago Sun-Times reached out to 10 Chicago area businesses in January 2025 and shared their plans and concerns for the future.</i><br><br><i>We’re now circling back, 12 months later, to see how they’ve fared.</i></div> </div><p>Chicago retailer and design studio Transit Tees entered 2026 with momentum and renewed purpose.</p><p>“It was a record-breaking year in 2024, and we improved on it in 2025,” Transit Tees owner Tim Gillengerten said. “It’s always great to have percentage gains from a best year.”</p><p>The gains showed up in-store and online, Gillengerten said. In December, retail sales at its Wicker Park and Andersonville shops were up 4% year over year, with online sales up 33%. </p><p>Gillengerten said much of the growth was from hiring more staffing dedicated to social media and digital marketing. He added a full-time and part-time employee focused on social media — an investment Gillengerten said paid off.</p><p>The company also increased its presence on Amazon, expanding from eight products to about 30, with plans to sell more.</p><p>Despite the strength of its online sales, Gillengerten said the brick-and-mortar stores remain central to the company's identity. Visitors often comment on the shop’s authenticity compared with the typical tourist shops, he said.</p><p>“The store is still a form of entertainment for a lot of people,” he said. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-720000" name="module-720000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Transit Tees</h3><br><b>About:</b> Gift shop specializing in Chicago-themed merchandise<br><b>Neighborhood: </b>Wicker Park and Andersonville<br><b>Owner:</b> Tim Gillengerten<br><b>Founded:</b> 2001<br><b>Staff size:</b> 23<br><b>Estimated annual revenue:</b> More than $1 million</div> </div><p>But he has noticed subtle changes in how customers spend.</p><p>“Wicker Park had an average sale of just over $50 per person ... and they bought an average of four products per visit,” he said. But those purchases were often for lower-priced items like stickers, magnets or small impulse buys.</p><p>“Stickers are actually our number one volume driver,” he said. </p><p>Chicago Transit Authority-stop magnets also sell in unusually high numbers. The accessibility of small items, like magnets and stickers,help shoppers “have fun, to spend a little money,” without hurting their budget during a time of inflation and economic anxiety, Gillengerten said.</p><p>Some higher-priced items — like custom LED light boxes made to look like the Old Style sign, as well as other Chicago iconography — sell well because they’re unique. Prices are about $130 to $220 for LED signs.</p><p>“If you have a custom item that you really can’t get anywhere else, those things will always have a lot of value and interest,” he said.</p> <p>Transit Tees has also seen a boost from trend-driven products tied to TV shows such as “The Bear," sports teams and moments in local culture.</p><p>Gillengerten said managing the day-to-day operations has grown more complex, especially with rising labor costs and the city's paid leave ordinance. The company employs 23 people — the most in its history.</p><p>As tariffs and material costs on supplies like poster paper rise, the company made a choice not to pass their increased costs on to customers.</p><p>“Our goal was to hold our pricing,” he said. “I just really wanted to hold the pricing and hope to sell more at this point, and we did.”</p><p>Instead, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2025/02/21/transit-tees-retail-gift-shop-rewards-creativity-love-chicago-wicker-park-andersonville" >Transit Tees</a> got creative. For example, when materials imported from overseas became too pricey, the team pivoted to locally-made items — including a 56-page coloring book created with the Chicago Architecture Center.</p><p>“It was printed in Chicago and designed here at our Wicker Park studio,” Gillengerten said.</p><p>Transit Tees typically introduces about 200 new products each year and maintains about 2,200 stock-keeping units. This year, the company is developing two new board games and several puzzles. It expects to release the new board games in the summer and fall. Developing a board game can be intensive and time-consuming — often taking nine months to a year to complete.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Our games sell more than anything else in the store,” Gillengerten said.</p><p>Last year, it launched a Portillo’s-themed Chicago-style hot dog puzzle, celebrating with a packed event at the restaurant’s Downtown location.</p><p>“We filled the entire second story with participants,” Gillengerten said, adding that there were contests for the fastest puzzle-builder and best-decorated hot dog.</p><p>Those types of community-building events and partnerships will expand significantly in 2026, Gillengerten said.</p><p>“There’s so many great Chicago companies,” he said. “It helps them because we have this authentic Chicago flair to our work.”</p><p>And he emphasized that their work and designs are original.</p><p>A social media user recently claimed a Transit Tees design was made by AI, and Gillengerten used the comment to share how the team makes its products.</p><p>“Our art directors have been adamant about not using AI for any of our products,” Gillengerten said. “We draw everything by hand. A lot of people don’t realize that.”</p><p>Looking ahead, he hopes for a strong small business ecosystem.</p><p>“A rising tide brings up all ships,” he said. “I want as many businesses for people to explore as possible. It helps us all.”</p><div class="PageListA" data-module ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-130001" name="list-130001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div entries.title: Dear Abby: Co-worker putting out quite the vibe in the office
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I work in an office with a man who has expressed an interest in me in various subtle (and not-so-subtle) ways, including winks. I have also caught him staring at me with intense longing. We communicate well, and he has told me I have influenced him. We are both disciplined, determined and accomplished men. I envy him for his easy smile, his self-confidence and his effortless, universal appeal.</p><p>This man is married with grown children. I am a widower, 21 years his senior. I am prepared to say to him, "You are spoken for, and I'm not a homewrecker. If you are ever single again, you and I should have a serious conversation. But unless that day comes, much will remain unsaid." I suspect the two of us would have a good life together. I do not believe I am misreading his intent. Have you any advice for me? — <i>INTERESTED IN WASHINGTON</i></p><p><b>DEAR INTERESTED:</b> This younger colleague may be as attracted to you as you are to him. However, he could also consider you nothing more than a valued mentor. Because he is married, I'm concerned that if you say what you are thinking to him, it could disrupt your working relationship or even be considered harassment. Do not jeopardize your job by doing it.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband vapes in our home, and I have run out of ways to get it through his thick skull that this is unhealthy, not only for his health, but for mine. Even more upsetting is that he vapes when our grandchildren are here and when he drives them in his truck.</p><p>I have printed reliable scientific information for him, yet his response is always, "I know, I'm sorry, I need to stop." It's been seven years, so that's how sincere his response is. He just continues trying to hide what he's doing.</p><p>I'm not wealthy enough to pack his bags and change the locks. But maybe someone will read this and smarten up before they damage their loved ones' health. Do you have any advice? — <i>THINKING ABOUT MY GRANDS</i></p><p><b>DEAR THINKING:</b> I do, actually. You imply that your husband is addicted to nicotine, which is why he is vaping. Tell him that you will quit nagging him about it on one condition: that when he vapes, he must step outside to do it, which will ensure that you are not affected by it. As to his vaping with the grands in the vehicle, how do their parents feel about it? Do they realize what Gramps is doing? Your next step should be to make sure they know exactly what is going on so they can put a stop to it.</p><p><b>DEAR READERS:</b> This is my timely reminder for all of you who live where daylight saving time is observed: Don't forget to turn your clocks forward one hour tonight at bedtime. Daylight saving time begins at 2 a.m. Sunday. I look forward to it each year because it signals longer, brighter days and warmer weather. I find the extra light to be a mood elevator and an energizer. Spring has almost sprung! — <i>LOVE, ABBY</i></p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For an excellent guide to becoming a better conversationalist and a more sociable person, order "How to Be Popular." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Popularity Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Richard Roeper’s 2026 Oscars predictions are here
entries.summary: <p>The 98th annual Academy Awards are a week away, so let’s look at some of the pressing questions about this year’s ceremony — and my picks in the four acting categories plus best picture.</p><p>I expect to see “One Battle After Another” and “Sinners” split the evening, with five total wins apiece; “Frankenstein” taking home as many as four Oscars in four craft categories; “KPop Demon Hunters” winning for best animated feature and original song; and nine other films/performances winning one Academy Award.</p><p>That so many of the major categories are still up for grabs this late should make for one of the most entertaining and unpredictable Oscars in recent years.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> Jacob Elordi as The Creature in a scene from “Frankenstein.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Netflix via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>What’s new or noteworthy about this year’s Oscars?</b></h3><p>These Academy Awards have already made history. With 16 nods, “Sinners” has set the record for the most nominations, besting “All About Eve” (1950), “Titanic” (1997) and “La La Land” (2016) with 14 apiece. They’ve also added best achievement in casting — the first new category since best animated feature was introduced in 2001.</p><h3><b>When does voting close for the Academy membership, and how will that impact the final tally?</b></h3><p>Voting for the 2026 Oscars began on Feb. 26 and closed Thursday at 5 p.m. Pacific Time. This is significant because many of the approximately 10,000 voters wait until the last day or two to cast their ballots. Momentum gained — or lost — from the Producers Guild Awards and Actor Awards can be a significant factor, especially in the case of the latter, as actors make up the largest voting bloc.</p><h3><b>Do any of this year’s nominees have Chicago ties?</b></h3><p>Supporting actress nominee Amy Madigan grew up in South Shore and graduated from (the now defunct) Aquinas High School. Her father was John Madigan, the legendary journalist and fixture at WBBM-Newsradio and CBS-2 Chicago. Also, Madigan played one of the all-time great girlfriends in one of the most beloved Chicago-area comedies ever, “Uncle Buck,” in which she was comedic gold opposite John Candy as the kind and patient but exasperated Chanice Kobolowski.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-420000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago native Amy Madigan arrives at the premiere of “Weapons” on Thursday, July 31, 2025, at The United Theater on Broadway in Los Angeles. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Richard Shotwell/Invision/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Can I bet on the Oscars?</b></h3><p>Yes and no. A number of platforms, including DraftKings and FanDuel, offer wagering on the Oscars in some states — but Illinois blocks awards betting on those sites. Illinois residents are allowed to wager on the Oscars via Kalshi, which offers contracts on the major and niche categories, as well as the specific number of wins for a specific film. For example, there’s a 16% chance “One Battle After Another” will win exactly five Oscars. If you place a $10 bet and it hits, you’d make a net profit of about $53. You can also speculate on “Who will attend the Oscars?” There’s a 99% chance Kylie Jenner will attend, which means that if you make a 99-cent investment, your return will be $1, congratulations! Ryan Gosling is at 61%, while Taylor Swift is a long shot to attend at 4% — so if you wagered $10 on Swift attending and she shows up, you’d realize a net profit of about $240. Friendly advice (from the guy who wrote a book titled “Bet the House: How I Gambled Over a Grand a Day on Sports, Poker and Games of Chance”): If you’re going to place a wager on the Academy Awards, limit it to the amount you’d spend on a popcorn and a drink at the movies. Enough to celebrate if you win, but not to feel pain if you don’t.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Netflix via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>What’s this I hear about the Oscars moving to YouTube? </b></h3><p>Starting in 2029 through 2033, YouTube will be the exclusive global platform for the Oscars, streaming the event live and free worldwide.</p><p>ABC has been the television home of the Academy Awards since the “One Flew Over the Cuckoo’s Nest” triumph at the 1976 ceremony. Last year, Hulu joined as a streaming partner. The ABC/Hulu pairing will continue through the spring of 2029, when YouTube will host the Academy Awards, becoming home base for arguably the biggest night in show business. In a world where Peacock and Prime Video carry NFL games and where Netflix, FX and HBO and AppleTV+ are forces at the prime-time Emmys, this move now seems almost inevitable.</p><p>Traditionally, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has been about as agile as the woolly mammoth when it comes to adapting to the times — but over the past decade, in the wake of reportage that found the voting membership was whiter, older and more male than the moviegoing public, not to mention the #MeToo and #OscarsSoWhite movements, the Academy launched a massive effort to diversify and expand membership, and to ensure a more inclusive nomination process.</p><p>Here are my predictions for who will win — and who <i>should</i> win — in five major categories (scroll to the bottom of this story for a complete, downloadable 2026 Oscars ballot, with Roeper's picks in bold):</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a Pictures via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Actress in a supporting role</b></h3><p><b>Will win:</b> Amy Madigan (“Weapons”)<br><b>Should win:</b> Amy Madigan<br><b>Contenders:</b> Teyana Taylor (“One Battle After Another”), Wunmi Mosaku (“Sinners”)</p><p>After Teyana Taylor’s Golden Globes win, she was the favorite — but Wunmi Mosaku’s win at the BAFTAs tightened the race, which became even more competitive with Amy Madigan’s name being called at the Actor Awards. By luck of the release schedule, this is one of the toughest competitions of the past 20 years. At 75, Amy Madigan is at least a generation older than the other nominees, and yes, sentiment sometimes sways the vote. Taylor and Mosaku left indelible impressions with their respective performances, but Madigan has the kind of flashy, over-the-top, utterly mad role that attracts attention. I mean, Gladys looks like Weird Barbie’s psychotic mom, yet Madigan somehow managed to make her more than a carnival sideshow.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Pictures via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Actor in a supporting role</b></h3><p><b>Will win:</b> Sean Penn (“One Battle After Another”)<br><b>Should win:</b> Delroy Lindo (“Sinners”)<br><b>In the running:</b> Stellan Skarsgård (“Sentimental Value”)</p><p>Yes, I’m hedging my bets — but this is another category where the race has taken some unexpected turns. Sean Penn’s attention-grabbing work as the grotesque Col. Steven J. Lockjaw in “One Battle After Another” has all the late juice going for it. (Penn has been one of our best actors for 40 years, but I felt his cartoonish performance took us out of the movie.) There’s a chance the universally respected Stellan Skarsgård will win for his finely calibrated work in “Sentimental Value” — but if I had a vote I’d cast it for Delroy Lindo, who is the grounded center and provides a sense of history amid all the dangerous, dreadful and violent chaos in “Sinners.” When Lindo’s world-weary Delta Slim spoke, it was as if the sound and fury around him was suddenly muted.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1b0000" name="image-1b0000"></a> Grzybowska/Focus Features via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Actress in a leading role</b></h3><p><b>Will win:</b> Jessie Buckley (“Hamnet”)<br><b>Should win:</b> Jessie Buckley<br><b>Other contenders:</b> None</p><p>You know the cliché: “It’s an honor just to be nominated.” In the best actress race, it’s quite true. For the enormously talented Rose Byrne (“If I Had Legs I’d Kick You”), Kate Hudson (“Song Sung Blue”), Renate Reinsve (“Sentimental Value”) and two-time winner Emma Stone (“Bugonia”), it’s the closest thing to a mathematical certainty that the nomination will be the reward. Jessie Buckley’s magnificent, searing and raw work in “Hamnet” is among the finest performances I’ve seen in any category this decade — and it ticks off a myriad of award-prestige boxes, as it’s an actor-driven, Shakespeare-inspired, deeply tragic period piece. It’s the kind of role and performance that draws in voters, and in this case, deservedly so.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure Pictures via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Actor in a leading role</b></h3><p><b>Will win:</b> Michael B. Jordan (“Sinners”)<br><b>Should win:</b> Michael B. Jordan<br><b>Primary contender:</b> Timothée Chalamet (“Marty Supreme”)</p><p>Timothée Chalamet comes across as a likable fellow who is clearly enjoying his ascension to stardom, and there’s absolutely nothing wrong with that. Chalamet is one of the most gifted actors of his generation, and until recently he appeared to be a lock to win best actor. Ah, but it’s a tricky thing when you appear to want something <i>too </i>much; to wit, Chalamet’s comment last year that he desired to be “one of the greats.” That might be turning off some voters. Chalamet lost at the BAFTAs in a major upset, with Robert Aramayo winning for “I Swear.” Then came the seismic moment at the Actor Awards when Michael B. Jordan won for his brilliant performance in “Sinners” — and the room erupted. At this point, it seems like a coin flip. I’m predicting Jordan will win based on the late surge of support. It was also my favorite piece of work in this group. Jordan gave us two distinct, unique, memorable characters in Smoke and Stack. The fact that one of them undergoes quite the transformation in the film almost makes this triple-character work. It’s a blazing, compelling, pure movie star performance.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Timothée Chalamet plays table tennis hustler Marty Mauser in “Marty Supreme.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div
